title: Setting up a kitchen caddy routine for food scraps
subtitle: Reducing mess, smell, and fruit flies while keeping a compost-ready caddy on the worktop
excerpt: A 5-litre caddy lined with a compostable liner holds 2-3 days of food scraps before it needs emptying. The main problems (smell and fruit flies) both have simple fixes: a tight-fitting lid, dry material layered between scraps, and emptying before the caddy gets more than two-thirds full.
sourceNotes: WRAP household food waste reduction guidance. Local authority food waste collection guidance (England, Wales). RHS composting guidance on kitchen waste inputs.

content.text: A kitchen caddy works best when it is emptied every 2-3 days and when dry material is used to absorb liquid between additions. A

makes the transfer clean. If you use a council food waste collection, use the liners specified by your council (some councils only accept certified compostable bags; others accept any bag). If you are taking scraps to your own compost heap, any paper or certified compostable liner works.
Fruit and vegetable peelings, cores, and tops
Tea bags (paper only; remove if they have a plastic mesh lining) and coffee grounds
Eggshells (these decompose slowly but add calcium)
Bread, cooked food, and meat (for council collection only, not a home compost heap)

Smell comes from anaerobic decomposition in the caddy. Keep the lid on at all times. After adding scraps, layer a small amount of dry material on top: torn cardboard, a sheet of newspaper, dry garden soil, or sawdust. This absorbs liquid and reduces the anaerobic layer at the bottom. Empty the caddy before it is more than two-thirds full: a full, heavy caddy is harder to tip cleanly and more likely to leak.

Fruit flies breed in the caddy if overripe fruit is left uncovered. The same dry-material layer (cardboard, newspaper) prevents flies from accessing the food surface. A caddy kept in a cool location (under the sink rather than on a warm worktop) slows down fly breeding. Empty more frequently in summer. Washing the caddy with hot soapy water after each liner change removes the residual liquid that attracts flies.

Kitchen scraps are nitrogen-rich (green material). When you empty the caddy onto the heap, bury the scraps under a layer of carbon-rich material (brown material): torn cardboard, autumn leaves, or wood chip. This covers odour, discourages pests, and maintains the carbon-to-nitrogen balance in the heap.
